story.title: Dr. Keisha Williams: Wildfire Detective
story.text:
My name is Dr. Keisha Williams. I am a Fire Behavior Analyst. I work for the U.S. Forest Service. My job is to predict where wildfires will go. I use science to keep people safe. I look at Earth's systems to make my predictions.

Every day, I check four main things.
1. I look at Rainfall. This is part of the hydrosphere, or Earth’s water. Less rain means plants get very dry.
2. I check the Dry Vegetation. This is the biosphere, or all living things. Dry plants are fuel for a fire.
3. I study the Wind. The wind is part of the atmosphere, or Earth’s air. Wind pushes fire and makes it grow.
4. I use this information to model Fire Spread. This helps firefighters know where to go.

My computer model is like a puzzle. I put in the pieces: wind, rain, and fuel. The model shows me a map of the fire's future path. But there is more to the puzzle. I also look at how steep the hills are. Fire moves much faster uphill. I also have to think about flying embers. Hot embers can start new fires far away. My job is to see the whole picture.
think_abouts: Dr. Williams called her model a 'puzzle'. What did she mean?
Why is it important for firefighters to know where a fire might spread?
story.go_deeper: What is one new part you could add to a fire model? Why would it be important?

margin_defs.definition: to say what will happen in the future
material like wood or plants that can burn
a computer program that shows how something works

check_prompts: What are two things Dr. Williams checks every day?
What is something else Dr. Williams adds to her model?

vocabulary.definition: The solid Earth—rocks, mountains, soil, and landforms
vocabulary.use_sentence: Fire moves faster uphill on the steep parts of the geosphere.

vocabulary.definition: All living things on Earth—plants, animals, fungi, bacteria
vocabulary.use_sentence: The dry plants of the biosphere become fuel for fires.

vocabulary.definition: All water on Earth—oceans, rivers, groundwater, ice
vocabulary.use_sentence: Less rain from the hydrosphere makes the land drier.

vocabulary.definition: The blanket of gases surrounding Earth—our air
vocabulary.use_sentence: Wind in the atmosphere pushes the fire across the land.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Williams looks at four Earth systems. How does the hydrosphere (water) affect the biosphere (plants) in her model?
discussion_cards.frame: The hydrosphere affects the biosphere because when there is less rain...

discussion_cards.prompt: Besides wind and dry plants, what is another thing that can make a fire spread faster?
discussion_cards.frame: Another thing that makes fire spread faster is...

discussion_cards.prompt: Why is a job like Dr. Williams's important for communities?
discussion_cards.frame: A Fire Behavior Analyst is important because they help...

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
